1.synthesis:
Judges 14:1–4 opens the Samson narrative proper with a scene that immediately establishes the tension that will run through all of chapters 14–16: Samson's attraction to a Philistine woman. 'Samson went down to Timnah, and at Timnah he saw one of the daughters of the Philistines. Then he came up and told his father and mother, "I saw one of the daughters of the Philistines at Timnah. Now get her for me as my wife"' (vv. 1–2). His parents' response invokes the covenant prohibition against intermarriage: 'Is there not a woman among the daughters of your relatives, or among all our people, that you must go to take a wife from the uncircumcised Philistines?' (v. 3). Samson's insistence — 'Get her for me, for she is right in my eyes' (v. 3) — uses the exact phrase the book's final verdict will use: 'everyone did what was right in his own eyes' (Judges 21:25). Samson is the embodiment of the judge-period's central problem.

The editorial note that follows is the passage's theological key: 'His father and mother did not know that it was from YHWH, for he was seeking an opportunity against the Philistines. At that time the Philistines ruled over Israel' (v. 4). YHWH is working through Samson's personal desire for a Philistine woman to create the conflict that will produce deliverance. The divine sovereignty operates through, not despite, Samson's compromised motivation.
1.historical_context: Timnah (modern Tell Batash) was approximately 6 km from Zorah in the Sorek Valley — Philistine-controlled territory at the edge of Dan's allotment. The intermarriage prohibition referenced by Samson's parents comes from Deuteronomy 7:3; the parents correctly identify the covenant violation. The editorial note in v. 4 is one of the most theologically significant editorial insertions in Judges — it reveals the divine perspective behind the human action. The narrator knows what the participants don't: YHWH is orchestrating this marriage arrangement for a purpose the marriage itself will not achieve directly.
1.literary_structure: The four-verse unit establishes the chapter's narrative premise: Samson wants a Philistine wife, his parents object, he insists, the editorial note reveals the divine purpose behind the human desire. The 'right in his eyes' language (v. 3) deliberately echoes the book's closing formula, making Samson not merely a character but a symbol of the era's spiritual condition.
1.theological_application: The 'YHWH was seeking an opportunity' note is one of the Bible's most direct statements about divine providence operating through human sin. Samson's desire is self-interested and covenant-violating; the outcome YHWH intends through it is Israel's deliverance. The permanent theological principle is that YHWH's sovereignty is not limited to working through the obedient actions of faithful people — he can and does work through the morally compromised decisions of flawed instruments to accomplish purposes those instruments did not intend. This does not excuse the covenant violation; it reveals the depth of divine resourcefulness.
1.christology: The divine purpose hidden within apparently compromised human action — 'they did not know that it was from YHWH' — is a pattern the NT names as the deep structure of the cross: 'Acts 2:23, this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men.' The most evil human act in history — the crucifixion — was simultaneously the most purposeful divine act. Samson's Philistine marriage, orchestrated by YHWH for a purpose no participant understood, is a small-scale anticipation of the pattern the cross enacts at maximum scale: divine sovereignty working through human failure to produce salvation.

5.synthesis:
Judges 14:5–9 narrates the two events that set up the riddle: the lion-killing and the honey from the carcass. As Samson travels to Timnah with his parents, separated from them in the vineyards, 'a young lion came toward him roaring. Then the Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and he tore the lion in pieces as one tears a young goat. He had nothing in his hand' (vv. 5–6). The Spirit-empowerment pattern — the same divine power that came on Othniel, Gideon, and others — produces in Samson an act of astonishing physical strength. But the location is significant: a vineyard. Samson the Nazirite — who is forbidden wine and grape products — is alone in a vineyard when the Spirit comes.

The honey episode follows on the return journey: Samson finds that bees have settled in the lion's carcass and made honey. He takes and eats it, and brings some to his parents 'but he did not tell them that he had taken the honey from the carcass of the lion' (v. 9). A Nazirite is forbidden contact with corpses (Numbers 6:6); Samson has already violated one Nazirite restriction by touching the lion's carcass, and now conceals this from his parents. The secret violation becomes the basis for the riddle he poses at his wedding — he turns his own covenant failure into entertainment.
5.historical_context: The Sorek Valley was known for its viticulture — the name 'Sorek' likely derives from shorek, a choice vine. Samson's Spirit-empowerment for physical feats is consistent with the judge pattern of charismatic empowerment for specific acts (Judges 3:10; Judges 6:34; Judges 11:29). The Nazirite prohibition of corpse-contact in Numbers 6:6–9 specifies that even accidental contact defiles the Nazirite and requires purification and restart of the vow period. Samson's deliberate use of the carcass honey — and his concealment of it — compounds the violation. The honey 'from the carcass' will become the riddle's answer: 'out of the eater came something to eat, out of the strong came something sweet.'
5.literary_structure: The unit is set up as a double irony: the Spirit comes upon Samson in a vineyard (a place forbidden to the Nazirite), and the victory produces a carcass Samson later desecrates. The very acts through which YHWH's Spirit works are simultaneously occasions for Samson's covenant failure. The two episodes — lion-killing and honey-taking — are both concealed from parents, making the reader privy to what the participants don't know.
5.theological_application: Samson's Spirit-empowerment occurring while he is already in covenant violation (alone in a vineyard) establishes one of the Samson cycle's most uncomfortable theological points: the Spirit of YHWH operates through instruments that are simultaneously divinely gifted and personally compromised. The Spirit's presence does not validate the Nazirite's covenant integrity; YHWH works through Samson's strength even when Samson is using his freedom to violate his vow. The permanent application is that the presence of spiritual gifts in a person's life does not certify their moral integrity — a sobering corrective to the assumption that gifted ministry indicates covenant faithfulness.
5.christology: The honey from the lion's carcass — 'out of the eater came something to eat, out of the strong came something sweet' — is a riddle that inverts expectation: the source of strength becomes the source of sweetness; the agent of death becomes the host of life. The pattern is a faint type of the resurrection: the one who 'destroyed the one who has the power of death' (Hebrews 2:14) becomes himself the source of life. Psalm 19:10 describes YHWH's law as 'sweeter than honey'; the lion whose strength was overcome produces honey — a small enactment of the principle that Christ's conquest of the strong man (Matthew 12:29) produces a sweetness that death itself could not have yielded without the victory.

10.synthesis:
Judges 14:10–20 narrates the wedding feast, the riddle, the betrayal, and the explosive aftermath. At the feast Samson poses his riddle to thirty Philistine companions: 'Out of the eater came something to eat. Out of the strong came something sweet' (v. 14). The stakes are thirty linen garments and thirty changes of clothes on each side; a seven-day deadline. When the Philistines cannot solve it, they pressure Samson's wife: 'Entice your husband to tell us the riddle, lest we burn you and your father's house with fire' (v. 15). She weeps and presses Samson for seven days; he capitulates and tells her; she tells the Philistines. Their solution — 'What is sweeter than honey? What is stronger than a lion?' (v. 18) — provokes Samson's famous rejoinder: 'If you had not plowed with my heifer, you would not have found out my riddle' (v. 18).

The Spirit of YHWH rushes on Samson again, and he goes to Ashkelon, kills thirty Philistines, takes their garments, and pays the wager — fulfilling the purpose YHWH was seeking: a provocation against the Philistines (v. 19). His wife is then given to his companion who had been his best man (v. 20). The chapter closes with Samson's anger, the further betrayal, and the chain of retaliations that will define the next chapter.
10.historical_context: The wedding feast format — seven days, thirty companions for the groom — reflects Philistine cultural practice. Philistine garments were a luxury item; thirty sets represented significant material wealth. The 'plowing with my heifer' idiom is the ancient Near Eastern equivalent of 'you used an insider source to get the answer.' Ashkelon was one of the five Philistine cities, approximately 35 km from Timnah — a day's journey. The Spirit-empowered killing of thirty Ashkelonites to pay the Timnah wager is one of the most uncomfortable Spirit-empowerment episodes in Judges: the Spirit comes to enable what is effectively a theft-through-murder to settle a gambling debt.
10.literary_structure: The unit escalates through riddle, threat, betrayal, and retaliation: the riddle posed (vv. 10–14), the threat to the wife (v. 15), the weeping and capitulation (vv. 16–17), the solution delivered (v. 18), Samson's violent payment (v. 19), the wife given away (v. 20). Each stage intensifies the conflict that will produce the chapters of retaliation.
10.theological_application: Samson's riddle-cycle reveals the pattern of personal failure that characterizes the entire judge-cycle at its most concentrated: a covenant person's secret sin (the carcass) becomes the basis for public entertainment (the riddle), produces betrayal by someone trusted (the wife), results in violent retaliation (Ashkelon), and leaves a trail of damaged relationships (the wife given away). The permanent application is that secrets about covenant failures rarely stay secret — they surface, often in contexts where the person who held them no longer controls the damage. Samson's attempt to profit from his own violation (the riddle wager) becomes the mechanism of his exposure.
10.christology: The wife's capitulation under threat — seven days of weeping until Samson tells her the secret, which she immediately passes to his enemies — is a small-scale anticipation of the Delilah pattern that will recur in chapter 16. Samson's pattern of trusting women who betray him to his enemies is the human narrative through which the divine purpose of Philistine provocation advances. The NT parallel is not flattering to the human instruments but accurate about the divine outcome: God working through broken human relationships and multiple betrayals to accomplish purposes that no participant intended. Paul's 'all things work together for good' (Romans 8:28) is not sentimentalism but the Samson-cycle's theological principle applied to those who love God.
